Vorscht's total elapsed time across the race was 89:07:41, earning her the first Grand Tour title of her professional career. paragraph: The Veldrana Altitude Race, a 96-kilometer mountain sportive contested at elevations between 2,100 and 3,600 meters across the Veldrana Spine ridge system, attracted 440 competitors on Saturday for its 11th edition, with the entire route above 2,000 meters throughout. Elite winner Nikolai Brenn of Strand-Ostheim Cycling completed the course in 3:22:14, averaging 28.5 km/h and cresting the highest point, Veldrana Col Nord at 3,598 meters, in a time of 2:04:11 from the race start — a col segment record by 3 minutes and 22 seconds. Women's winner Renata Ohlvik arrived in 3:41:08, also setting a women's segment record on the Veldrana Col Nord by 4 minutes and 11 seconds. The event's compulsory medical pre-screening, introduced in 2021 following an altitude sickness incident, cleared 98.6 percent of applicants to race, with six riders assessed as unsuitable for the altitude profile based on resting blood oxygen saturation below 94 percent at the sign-on venue in the valley town of Altenholt, situated at 1,800 meters. The Veldrana Altitude Race carries 150 prestige points in the Mountain Events Challenge series and is the highest-rated event in the series calendar.

paragraph: Six months after suffering a fractured clavicle in a crash at the Veluron Classic, Fabiola Wern of Team Percheron-Lux made her return to racing at the Drechen Criterium, finishing an encouraging seventh in the 60-minute circuit race around Drechen's historic centre. Her return to racing, at a race average speed of 46.8 km/h over the 60-minute criterium, was described by Percheron-Lux directeur sportif Mireille Vant as ahead of the team's schedule by approximately two weeks. Wern's next scheduled appearance is the Caldren Valley stage race in three weeks, where she is expected to ride in a support role before attempting solo breakaway opportunities in the event's mountain stages. paragraph: Course conditions at the Drevlan Mountain Ultramarathon deteriorated severely on race day after 48 hours of persistent rain saturated the 86-kilometer route through the Drevlan Highlands, transforming the course's three major creek crossings into thigh-deep torrents requiring swimmer-assisted crossings. Race director Halvard Noss activated the event's emergency weather protocol 4 hours into the race, deploying 12 additional safety volunteers to the creek crossings and shortening the course by 14 kilometers via a pre-approved diversion that bypassed the Malvern Gorge section rated highest-risk under the flood assessment. Despite the conditions, 74 of the 96 starters reached the finish of the modified 72-kilometer course, with winner Goran Telvach of Solvent Trail Athletics completing it in 9:02:44. Women's winner Lindwe Dassier finished in 10:18:33. Post-race analysis showed that the diversion added 340 meters of ascent but removed the most technically challenging descent of the original route. Noss confirmed that the prize money and course records will apply to the modified distance, a decision ratified by the Drevlan Athletics Federation within 48 hours of the race conclusion.

paragraph: The Korvex Pro Cycling women's squad announced a complete roster rebuild for the upcoming season, retaining only three of its eight previous riders and signing five new recruits from six different nations. paragraph: The 18-stage Grand Tour de Aurveld came to a close on Sunday with Alonso Ferrat of Team Carvento-Dumas claiming his second consecutive overall title, finishing the 3,188-kilometer race with a total time of 79:22:11 and a winning margin of 4 minutes and 38 seconds over runner-up Davu Mwenye of Korvex Pro Cycling. paragraph: Seventeen teams from across the professional women's road circuit gathered in the lakeside city of Lauvern for the start of the Lauvern Lakes Three-Day Race, a 402-kilometer stage race combining a flat opening stage, a lake-circuit criterium, and a decisive 194-kilometer mountain conclusion. The Lauvern Three-Day Race carries 400 prestige points and represents the third most valuable women's stage race in the regional calendar.

paragraph: Scientists from the Veldora Institute of Sports Science published findings from a two-year study of pacing strategy at the Korvath 200K Ultra, analysing GPS and heart-rate data from 247 finishers across three race editions. The study identified three distinct pacing archetypes: the even-pace cohort (31 percent of finishers) who maintained speed within 8 percent of their mean throughout, the positive-split cohort (46 percent) who faded progressively after the 100-kilometre mark, and the negative-split cohort (23 percent) who ran the second half faster. Crucially, the negative-split cohort achieved finishing times an average of 48 minutes faster than the positive-split cohort despite identical first-half velocities, suggesting that restraint in the early stages was the most significant performance determinant. Course record holder Goran Telvach's record run was identified as a textbook negative-split execution, with his second 100 kilometres covered 19 minutes faster than his first. Researchers recommended that race briefings include explicit pacing guidance for the Thorn Canyon section at kilometre 88, where the positive-split cohort typically collapsed, averaging a 23 percent speed reduction compared to the negative-split cohort's 4 percent reduction over the same 14-kilometer technical section.
